The crazy version of Sharing Stories

People get funny when you say you want to share examples and stories. They get nervous. They start second guessing and over-thinking.

Michael walked into his office on…

When Rachel was called into her manager’s office on a Friday…

Samantha was taken aback and taken down for a ride when Bill said…

The moment they walked in, when they got up from their chairs, the way they sat down…

Too much. Too much. Too much. Too much talking.

This is the kind of fine tuning I reserve for my comedy monologues. It can be fun. But I would rather generate a sample set or two of sample stories.

When we put ourselves in our heads, lay out the key points and data, and get up and start speaking in front of the story we have created, we build a solid foundation for delivering engaging lab practices.

Sharing stories in practical lab situations
In the past two years, Sharing Stories has become the best way to manage my teaching. By actually saying the first words of my story, I kick-start my performance drive. From there, bit by bit, I generate each story, read it out loud and deliver it.

Sharing Stories has become what Paul Sherman Kundzicz would call a "high-engagement approach for learning facilitator preparation"^1

It alleviates paralysis by overthinking. It gets me out of my own head, out of my own way. For me it's quicker to build a scenario of positive workplace feedback when given a real situation like the one I had to manage where I had to tell a senior director the project was going to be completed a month late. I performed this online to the students in my class, who saw the benefit of this in the preparation of their own labour market orientation report.

Sharing stories is worth learning. Here are the five-fold benefits:
a. Laboratory folks are learning facilitators. We have to tell stories to kids in labs. We do this first by preparing to share stories, and then to deliver them.

How Do You Create Scenarios?

Generating Scenarios

Having read all these great stories, you may wonder where all these great managers and employees came from. The answer as most things in management is: from practice. Combining what and how much to practice depends in part on you and your learning style.

Just like writing a good book, good scenario development takes meticulous planning and lots of red pen editing. It may be easier if you put off working directly in the project management software for a while, and instead begin writing your story directly into the template.

Take your time. Create a story that has a beginning, middle and end. Avoid working on specific actions for now, because you will have to break these actions down into sequences, sequence steps and processes. Telling your story in this way will enable you to get started thinking of the project: How and why it will be done, who is involved, member roles, etc.

Good stories don't need to be printed or saved in the computer. Save time by putting your scenarios together in stages. The good news is that you can present all these stories in the form of files once you are done adding the elements to your template.

It is best if you create your own templates, but our templates also include various reading materials available free on Business.govt.nz. All you need to do is copy and paste your scenario into the template.

Blank forms feature: Reiterability and customization

The forms that ShipNotify uses are simple and easy to customize. My download included customizations that were based on other types, so they had well-commented code and easy-to-understand tables of the field definitions within the forms. All three scenarios I downloaded included separate forms for adjusting the date of a shipment and for showing a brief summary of shipments. Each scenario had a text-only version that I could focus on learning the details of the scenario without my browser bogging down. I noticed that the form definitions specified that gallery views and collection views would update automatically, so I could easily display the data from the forms in a grid view or on a map without coding.

Blank forms feature: Reusable data

ShipNotify includes a sample data set, however, you have to download three different SKUs to import them through AngularJS. For me, that was too much back and forth between my development and test environment.

In an epiphany, I realized that I could search for a shipping solution and get data about a SKU from the public body and set it as the data in my LocalStorage or Cookies. Thus, if I were to use a third-party solution that used a local database, I wouldn't have to import data; it would be instantly available. I discovered immediately that eBay's eBay Commerce Network implemented the integration pattern in shopatron.js. One way I could use this would be to set up a JavaScript file based on the ecommerce model defined in AngularJS and include in the answer to the question "How can I use this as a base stock management demo that would provide shipping data and order management data for all sorts of scenarios?". Oh, and I'd love to create a form that I could use to quickly and easily move stock between locations!

One source I always consider to be trustworthy is local communities within the InfoSphere and IoT story. This solution included the group of domain experts (from the Europe, Africa and Middle East region) explaining how to import stock from an XSLT file. Up to that point, all the work I had done with the solutions had been able to update the data through Excel, WebSphere Commerce, or a REST API, with all of them being a bit cumbersome. Ah, but this!

Activity: Lossgap intimacy strategy—Instant Price Incaon question & response

In a customer service call, voice mail, email, sending a price right away, returns using a flat card process) customers often do not share information about their challenges and needs, they just want to know a price. The temptation then is to send the prices, and hoping the customer will share information, or that one day the customer will call back.

The more likely result is the customer ceases communication altogether.

Facilitating Triggers for Social Change

Our research shows us that facilitating social change spans a range of topics. I have fun social research charts to graphically illustrate social change triggers.

Supporting Change Triggers - Incremental

The Support Triggers are generated from within the group and it takes all members in the group to pull off the action together. Some supporting triggers include collective sense of identity, confidence in the group members and their broader support network.

It takes trust, perception of common objectives and team members to be able to support change by coming together to pull things off together. It is about collective motivation to support a trigger. All of the individual members cannot bring about change generated from within - e.g. brainstorming or idea generation.

Scenario: Manager calls employee into his office for a performance evaluation
Story: On a brisk Autumn day, Bill was called into his manager's office. The manager looked grim and stated that Bill's quarterly performance evaluation was not good. Bill's comments that he was trying his best and that he expected positive results were met coolly, at best, and coldly at worst. Bill then felt himself in serious trouble. It turned out that his boss was an avoider—that is, she did not like giving negative feedback.

Diagram 4.6. Performance Evaluation Storyboard

Story 2: Negative Feedback
Praise is good for morale and improves productivity, resulting in happier employees and better relationships. But, on occasion, employees need the negative feedback. Negative feedback is by no means negative happiness. Personnel managers provide negative feedback when needed through illustrations of success or failure. Several of these stories follow. Workplace stories about manager-initiated feedback from peers, customers, co-workers, and other managers.

CHAPTER SUMMARY
Feedback and participating are two important elements for building effective communication between vendors and customers as well as employees and their managers.

- Feedback is an acknowledgment, or security signal, from production or service delivery to a vendor and from the vendor to the customer. Positive feedback strengthens the system. Negative feedback provides information to modify the system.
- Feedback can be direct or indirect. Direct feedback means that information is about a person, and indirect feedback is about a situation.
- The presentation of feedback is important to how it is perceived within your firm. Using examples, stories, studies, and metaphor is more helpful to an employee's development than merely stating or providing information.
- Many times it is not known how much information is provided. Providing some background and context about the feedback makes it easier to understand.

Stop for a moment and think back to a time when someone gave you feedback. How did the feedback feel? Was it unexpected? Is there something you would like to change about that experience?

Now think about a time when you were able to provide feedback to someone. How did the recipient respond? Is there something you would like to change about that experience?

Consider what we know about giving and receiving feedback and see if this information can add any value to your experiences.

Although I can see a case for preferring “I was told by my boss” in this case, I would find “I” a valid alternative that conveys the same meaning as “My boss told me”.

The plain “I” puts the emphasis on the speaker, whereas “my boss” focuses on the person spoken to, and not the speaker. The last three sentences of the question and its answer do not occur in the book. As far as I know in the book all three characters are female, so “my boss” is probably less appropriate in the circumstances than “I”, in the view of the author.

A recent situation at work inspired me to write about this, in how managers need to learn this:

With a new project that had just gone live, I received many sympathizing comments from 2 co-workers who shared that they were impacted negatively by this project, that it has made their lives more difficult. I chose to create a new story (starting with “In this story we follow me, who, after being so very critical of how much work was required from everyone to launch this new project, now realize, I was wrong to think all that extra work for everyone to impute data into the new project, is counter-productive”)

The manager who called Michael to his office created a memorable self-empowering and enriching moment for him to grow, to celebrate his successes and accomplishments. We can draw many parallels to our own personal and professional lives because we’ve done similar things to and for others. When we take the time to remember such enriching moments, we too share and grew from Michael’s story.

I am still teaching little ones, and from past experience, I've realized that it's good to praise the child within ourselves for big or small victories in our life. No matter how small the event, it tends to make our spirit soar and brighten our face if we focus on the good that came out of it. How we face the situation give us a very strong positive or negative emotional reaction at the time. With this kind of encouragement, we are able to pour most of our positive energy into the new situation.

Where would you most like to receive your operation bonus...the hubba hubba hub or the flower shop?
I raise the question of how much you choose to get involved in cultivating your employee relations, because usually as managers we're called upon to deliver good news, such as when we're handing out bonuses, invitations to membership in management clubs or grants of promotions. It's also part of our daily work, however, to provide corrective feedback.

It doesn't make sense to me that we invest so much energy and material resources in moving from the follow the leader to action learning models of meetings, wherein we empower individuals to act as reflective collaborators while the discussion is happening. We advocate the increase of accountability and the empowerment of those on teams to make decisions for themselves. Instead, what happens when a colleague doesn't meet a milestone or deliver within a budget? We become really uncomfortable and fail to perform even appropriately because we start to second guess ourselves as to our own approaches. We wait for guidance.

The principal with whom I used to work when building these collaborative meetings describes the severe lack of trust such methods engender, because if we need the manager to provide us with the corrective information from the employee, halfway to the slide we no longer believe the employee's response. Delivering feedback is a core competency of a manager, yet it is done in almost a vacuum so we empower the employee to a point, and then at the next milestone, we have no idea what it is that employee is doing behind our back.

Rather than follow this pattern, I've formulated a technique that has helped empower employees to take liberties with the messaging and guidance we deliver from our perspective. It goes like this: first, I address my statements as if the employee is already watching their actions from my perspective, that they already know how 'that' piece that they're working on fits into the bigger picture or how 'that' part of the performance appraisal is going to affect everyone's true ability to share compensation from their work. Second, when I want to address a critical issue or rest a performance issue, I review the story I plan to tell with the employee to their face and ask for permission to share that story with the boss. Study results
Along with MBA students and professionals, the study looked at college-level students who have had minimal work experience (60 percent, 25 percent had 1 to 2 years and 13 percent had 3 or more years). Other findings include:

- College students who receive more positive feedback tend to think less often about changing jobs than those who receive more negative or neutral feedback, while positive feedback is no hindrance among professionals.
- College students who received positive feedback rated their immediate supervisors more highly and were more likely to recommend their organization for employment than those receiving less positive feedback.
- MBA students who receive more positive feedback from their supervisors rated their current work experience more highly and were more positive about their future job prospects than those who received more neutral or negative feedback.
- College students who received more feedback overall rated their immediate supervisor more positively and were more likely to recommend their organization for employment than those who did not receive more feedback overall.
- College students who received more negative feedback were more likely to consider leaving their organization than were those who received more neutral or positive feedback.
- MBA students who received more feedback overall stated that they are less likely to consider leaving their organization than those who received less feedback overall.
- MBA students who received more negative feedback were as likely to consider leaving their organization as were those who received more positive or neutral feedback.

Taken together, these findings appear to indicate that employees view feedback as an investment – both in the employer and in themselves. So, what meaning should managers make of this? The authors, Amy Newman-Grant, Janet Harrison, and Paul Moran, cited by Ariens (2013), found that the managers who "create a culture that values feedback, be that positive, negative or otherwise, will often discover that employees who feel valued in turn value the organization, which ultimately affects organizational retention rates." Overview

Benefits Realization Best Practices We Learn From Project Managers

Barry LePatner AIA in Contract Management Magazine | Contract Management | Contract Management today describes how two conflicting trends in the A/E industry can be resolved with benefits realization management (BRM) in his new article, A Creative New Allocation Consensus That Works. Key points include:

- "There are two conflicting trends in the A/E industry. One of them is the emergence and popularity of design build (D/B), the other is benefit realization management (BRM)."
- "It turns out that the two processes go hand-in-glove. If you want the best outcome, then you need to change the way your firm manages the process, and you also have to change dramatically how you procure the work."
- "The key goes back to standards, specifically those imposed on the D/B process. "We've performed much of this innovation within our D/B practice's office of program management. We've also generated valuable lessons learned from our work to help private sector clients successfully manage large, complex projects. As a result, we've identified some critical components necessary for establishment of an successful BRM practice."

The author identifies five best practices gleaned from the experiences of the office of program management:

1. Establish metrics specific to the benefits generated by each project from the very start. You need to monitor the alignment of the effort with the payback.

2. Establish benefits realization metrics for each individual. Consider establishing pay-for-value tie-ins to benefits.

3. Establish metrics for the entire firm related to overall benefits realization achievement.

4. Link social charts to development of compensation plans - compensation plan components tied to ownership interests, project performance, and firm contribution.

5. The firm-wide benefits realization plan provides a significant enhancement from the traditional project moay human resources plan. Reallocating resources based on the firm-wide consequences of job performance expands individual and firm maturity beyond project contribution scope and includes deliverables to the firm itself.

When I re-read my own workplace Memoir for the first time, I was surprised by the impact of this simple detail. It reawakened in me strong ideas about what causes positive feelings - and how those feelings affect our reasoning and judgement styles.

Over the walk to work on that brisk September morning, I began to reflect on how different the two versions of the event I had just read actually are. When Andy smirks or smiles, his positive affect clearly contaminates the feedback mechanics in a way that Rachel's cold facial indifference doesn't.

What happens if the same thing happens to these employees Monday morning? Again, the two scenes will be identical in detail, yet the mood of the manager's office at the beginning - or the "mood parameter" of the story - has a massive effect on the way we read and understand those events.

Great Employee Feedback Has More Elements (Part 1)

by Murat Mutlu Oct 26, 2011

Although one can argue that there is a lot of spin in writing about employee engagement, it is true that employee engagement is one of the most interesting subjects facing managers these days. People leadership, as a subject, is more relevant than ever in organizations that are faced with the challenge and necessity of changing/transforming their ways of operating in order to survive and prosper. And to not only survive, for competitive advantage, but to grow and beat the competition as we are experiencing during present economic conditions.

One vital aspect of employee engagement is the desire for (and proactively providing support for) development by means of feedback from peers and the beauty of this is that the amount of objectivity provided can be amplified by means of effective feedback processes such as Officevibe’s Employee Pulse Check process.

One can see restlessness for the feedback emerging that will allow employees to work effectively at their preferred style of expression. And it is here that employee engagement really can help the management team drive change in the way that an organization interacts with employee talent and enable the employees to be a part of the process itself. Two questions come to mind here:

1. What is a type of feedback that enables the flow of natural talent?

2. What type of feedback becomes and becomes always behavioral?

In a nutshell, effective feedback is read, interpreted and internalized before it has any impact. Additionally, when employees are engaged in the process, the chance of them when tested, taking up the standards shared with them improves.

In order to answer the first question; we have to ask ‘the kind of feedback’ that we would all like to see. Most people, if they want to have any idea of self-improvement should be on the lookout for Garcia’s Four Types of Feedback in 1992 which is on the continuum of positive/negative/repeated/insults as shown below. Garcia tried to find a way to categorize input elements that we receive from others and identified that there are three basic types of feedback we can receive on positive/negative issues and that feedback is either repeated or it is just once given. Condensed version:

Decide how you will collect data
There are numerous ways employees can be prompted to provide feedback, including offering stationary anonymous suggestion boxes, asking stakeholders to fill in paper surveys or creating custom solutions like web forms.

Any method can be effective in delivering a regular stream of customer feedback, as long as your customers have the option to share their feedback privately and securely.

Regardless on what method you decide to choose, ensure that it will deliver actionable data to help your organization improve and keep on supporting customers better.

Consider site behaviour and visit patterns
To collect actionable customer feedback successfully, it is important to understand how visitors behave on your website.

We define a visitor flow as the path a visitor goes through on your website, from arrival point to leaving.

A simple example of a visitor flow is a new visitor arriving on your ‘Content page’. They then proceed with reading this piece of content before they decide to leave.

Visitor flows can differ substantially when it comes to the paths visitors take on a website. Depending on visitors' interests, the content they view or the products they are looking to buy this can change relatively quickly.

For instance, a workflow could look like this:

A visitor arrives from a marketing email; this visitor starts the survey about their opinion of the new promotion

Once completed, the visitor then goes back to the website and completes a purchase

During the purchase process, they encounter an upsell opportunity, which they purchase

Knowing the visitor flow on your website helps companies to improve their marketing collateral based on the specific needs of customers, which are highlighted by the survey responses.

This also ensures that customers do not feel pressured into answering questions that are not relevant or simply going through the motions.

Use multivariate testing to get the full picture
Using multivariate testing, you can identify source issues that may be impacting the efficacy of your surveys.

Multivariate testing allows you to test properties relevant to performance. The only difference is that you don't have to stop after defining one test. You define as many tests as you want, until you get the best data.

Stating the factors that need to be tested is easy; the process of creating these is not. Multivariate testing can be broken down by experimentation.

historia:
Madeline, who was summoned to her supervisor's office after lunch. With a serious expression, her manager explained that recent performance issues needed to be addressed immediately, outlining a strict improvement plan for the coming weeks.

The suggestions offered include:

1. Have feedback delivered when it will minimally distract or disrupt
2. Always discuss strengths and accomplishments before moving to weaknesses and areas for growth
3. Have a definite Action Plan in mind before beginning
4. Before scheduling a Feedback Session, set measurable objectives for the Discussion
5. Put objectives, together with a strategy for achieving them, in writing
6. A manager needs to sell, rather than tell
7. Deliver feedback using constructive, supporting, and non-offensive language
8. Feedback - whether positive or negative - can be based on actions, such as continuously exceeding goals.
9. Employees deserve respect in the same way managers do. This practice must be identified and adhered to in every interaction including Feedback Sessions for the most positive results. When trust and respect develops, employees follow their manager's lead willingly, and performance is self-generating.
10. Finally, remember to acknowledge employee success, however small it may seem.

In Summary

One of the most powerful elements of really listening is understanding the source of the communication in the first place.

For what it’s worth: Communication research tells us that other areas should also be considered when giving feedback.

“Thy neighbor shall be to thee as a brother” (Proverbs 27:6). Our communities are as crucial to our well being as is our workplace.

Jan Frühbeck is a professor of Human Resources at the University of Gothenburg, Sweden.

Entre suspenses e frases cortantes, Carla compreende que aquele momento marcará uma virada em sua trajetória profissional, forçando-a a encarar falhas e desafios que até então preferia ignorar.
